perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be deemed to made in and in all respects shall be interpreted, construed, and governed by and in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be deemed to made in and in all respects shall be interpreted, construed, and governed by and in accordance with the laws of the State of Montana without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Delaware to Montana could impact the enforceability of the non-compete agreement, as Delaware law is generally more favorable to such agreements than Montana law. The original contract was signed in relation to a company in Montana, so changing the law to Montana would imply that it should have been applied initially, which is not true.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law offers more latitude in enforcing non-compete agreements compared to Montana. By stating that Montana law governs the agreement, it implies a weakening of the non-compete clause that was originally intended to be governed by Delaware law, reducing its enforceability given the standards applied in Delaware. This is further reinforced by the company being located in Montana but initially being governed by Delaware.

(a) All bonds, specialties, and notes in writing, payable to any person, or order, or assigns, may be assigned, or indorsed, and the assignee, or indorsee, his or her executors, administrators, or assigns, may again assign, or indorse the same, as often as desired. The assignees, or indorsees, or their executors, or administrators, may, in their own name, sue for and recover the money due on the bonds, specialties, or notes. All assignments of bonds or specialties shall be under hand and seal, and executed before at least one credible witness. (b) Assignors, or indorsers, or their executors, or administrators may not release or discharge any sum due by the bonds, specialties, or notes, after the date of the assignment, and no release, receipt, or discharge from him, her or them made after the date of the assignment shall be good or available. perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Delaware to Montana introduces a legal contradiction because it weakens the enforceability of the non-compete agreement. The perturbation explanation clearly states that Delaware law is generally more favorable to such agreements than Montana law. The law explanation reinforces this by stating that by stating that Montana law governs the agreement, it implies a weakening of the non-compete clause that was originally intended to be governed by Delaware law, reducing its enforceability given the standards applied in Delaware. This alteration undermines the original intent and enforceability of the contract, creating a structural flaw that leads to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All notices required or permitted to be given under this Agreement shall be given by certified mail, return receipt requested, to the parties at the following addresses or such other addresses as any party may designate in writing to the other parties: If to Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition: 5759 Fleet Street, Suite 220 Carlsbad, California 92008 Attention: President and Chief Executive Officer with a copy (which shall not constitute notice) to: Gordon E. Forth, Esq. Woods Oviatt Gilman LLP 700 Crossroads Building 2 State Street Rochester, New York 14614 If to Principal: 101 Sourdough Ridge Road Bozeman, Montana 59715
perturbation.changed_text: All notices required or permitted to be given under this Agreement shall be given by carrier pigeon. If to Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition: 5759 Fleet Street, Suite 220 Carlsbad, California 92008 Attention: President and Chief Executive Officer with a copy (which shall not constitute notice) to: Gordon E. Forth, Esq. Woods Oviatt Gilman LLP 700 Crossroads Building 2 State Street Rochester, New York 14614 If to Principal: 101 Sourdough Ridge Road Bozeman, Montana 59715
perturbation.explanation: Requiring all notices to be sent via carrier pigeon is not commercially reasonable and introduces ambiguity and uncertainty regarding receipt of the notices. This challenges the enforceability of provisions contingent upon proper notification.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General contract law principles regarding reasonable notice
perturbation.law_explanation: Due process, and thus enforceable contract law, requires 'reasonable notice' which means the method of notification must be practical and likely to inform the intended recipient. The use of 'carrier pigeon' for official notifications contradicts the requirement of reasonable notice due to its unreliability, lack of tracking, and susceptibility to external factors, making it difficult to prove that notice was properly given and received. This is especially important in legal notices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: due process | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you due process What is due process? Due process (or due process of law) primarily refers to the concept found in the Fifth Amendment to the US Constitution, which says no one shall be "deprived of life, liberty or property without due process of law" by the federal government. The Fourteenth Amendment , ratified in 1868, uses the same eleven words, called the Due Process Clause, to extend this obligation the the states. These words have as their central promise an assurance that all levels of American government must operate within the law ("legality") and provide fair procedures. There are at least three other uses of the phrase "due process" in American constitutional law, and this article will also touch on each briefly. Incorporation The Fifth Amendment's reference to “due process” is only one of many promises of protection the Bill of Rights gives people against the federal government. Originally these promises had no application at all against the states; the Bill of Rights was interpreted to only apply against the federal government, given the debates surrounding its enactment and the language used elsewhere in the Constitution to limit State power. (see Barron v City of Baltimore (1833)). However, this changed after the enactment of the Fourteenth Amendment and a string of Supreme Court cases that began applying the same limitations on the states as the Bill of Rights. Initially, the Supreme Court only piecemeal added Bill of Rights protections against the States, such as in Chicago, Burlington & Quincy Railroad Company v. City of Chicago (1897) when the court incorporated the Fifth Amendment's Takings Clause into the Fourteenth Amendment. The Court saw these protections as a function of the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment only, not because the Fourteenth Amendment made the Bill of Rights apply against the states. Later, in the middle of the Twentieth Century, a series of Supreme Court decisions found that the Due Process Clause "incorporated" most of the important elements of the Bill of Rights and made them applicable to the states. If a Bill of Rights guarantee is " incorporated " in the "due process" requirement of the Fourteenth Amendment, state and federal obligations are exactly the same. For more information on the incorporation doctrine, please see this Wex Article on the Incorporation Doctrine . Substantive due process The words “due process” suggest a concern with procedure rather than substance, and that is how many—such as Justice Clarence Thomas, who wrote "the Fourteenth Amendment’s Due Process Clause is not a secret repository of substantive guarantees against unfairness" —understand the Due Process Clause. However, others believe that the Due Process Clause does include protections of substantive due process—such as Justice Stephen J. Field, who, in a dissenting opinion to the Slaughterhouse Cases wrote that "the Due Process Clause protected individuals from state legislation that infringed upon their ‘privileges and immunities’ under the federal Constitution” (see this Library of Congress Article: https://www.loc.gov/exhibits/magna-carta-muse-and-mentor/due-process-of-law.html ) Substantive due process has been interpreted to include things such as the right to work in an ordinary kind of job, marry, and to raise one's children as a parent. In Lochner v New York (1905), the Supreme Court found unconstitutional a New York law regulating the working hours of bakers, ruling that the public benefit of the law was not enough to justify the substantive due process right of the bakers to work under their own terms. Substantive due process is still invoked in cases today, but not without criticism (See this Stanford Law Review article to see substantive due process as applied to contemporary issues). The promise of legality and fair procedure Historically, the clause reflects the Magna Ca
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by mandating 'carrier pigeon' for all notices, directly contradicts the requirement of 'reasonable notice' implied within due process. The Law Explanation clearly states that 'carrier pigeon' introduces unreliability and lacks tracking, undermining the assurance that all levels of American government must operate within the law and provide fair procedures, as emphasized in Scraped Law Snippet 2. The change removes a commercially reasonable form of communication, impacting the enforceability of provisions. The original text implied reasonable method by using certified mail.

perturbation.original_text: Principal acknowledges and agrees that Phoenix Footwear and PFG Acquisition will suffer irreparable harm in the event that Principal breaches any of its obligations under this Agreement, and that monetary damages shall be inadequate to compensate Phoenix Footwear and PFG Acquisition for any such breach. Principal agrees that in the event of any breach or threatened breach by Principal of the provisions of this Agreement, Phoenix Footwear and PFG Acquisition, or either of them, shall be entitled to a temporary restraining order, preliminary injunction, and permanent injunction in order to prevent or restrain any such breach or threatened breach by Principal, or by any or all of Principal's agents, representatives or other persons directly or indirectly acting for, on behalf of, or with Principal.
perturbation.changed_text: Principal acknowledges and agrees that Phoenix Footwear and PFG Acquisition *may* suffer irreparable harm in the event that Principal breaches any of its obligations under this Agreement, and that monetary damages *may* be inadequate to compensate Phoenix Footwear and PFG Acquisition for any such breach. Principal agrees that in the event of any breach or threatened breach by Principal of the provisions of this Agreement, Phoenix Footwear and PFG Acquisition, or either of them, shall be entitled to request a temporary restraining order, preliminary injunction, and permanent injunction in order to prevent or restrain any such breach or threatened breach by Principal, or by any or all of Principal's agents, representatives or other persons directly or indirectly acting for, on behalf of, or with Principal.
perturbation.explanation: Weakening the language from 'will suffer irreparable harm' to 'may suffer irreparable harm' and from 'shall be entitled' to 'request' weakens the enforceability of the injunctive relief clause. This change introduces uncertainty about whether irreparable harm will actually occur and whether injunctive relief will be granted, making it more difficult for Phoenix Footwear and PFG Acquisition to obtain an injunction.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 65 outlines the requirements for obtaining injunctive relief. A key element is demonstrating a likelihood of irreparable harm if the injunction is not granted. By weakening the acknowledgement of irreparable harm from 'will' to 'may', and changing 'shall be entitled' to 'request', the agreement undermines the strength of Phoenix Footwear and PFG Acquisition's case for injunctive relief. Courts require a clear showing of irreparable harm to grant an injunction. The 'may' instead of 'will' would suggest that the harm is speculative and not certain, going against the precedent set by the case law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. Read the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure (PDF) Related Downloads Federal Rules of Civil Procedure 431.61 KB Return to top Your browser does not support Javascript. Your browser does not support Javascript.
perturbation.justification: The original text provided strong assurance of 'irreparable harm' and entitlement to injunctive relief, while the changed text weakens this to 'may' suffer harm and a request for relief. The Law Explanation states that courts require a 'clear showing of irreparable harm' to grant an injunction. By changing 'will' to 'may', the agreement suggests the harm is speculative, making it harder to obtain injunctive relief, as noted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, regarding Rule 65, mentions 'immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage' in the context of temporary restraining orders, indicating the importance of demonstrating irreparable harm. The change weakens the claim of irreparable harm and the entitlement to injunctive relief, creating a structural flaw that directly contradicts the legal basis for obtaining an injunction.

perturbation.original_text: The covenants set forth in this Section 2 shall be construed as a series of separate covenants covering their subject matter in each of the separate states within the -2- <PAGE> Territory and, except for geographic coverage, each such separate covenant shall be deemed identical in terms to the covenant set forth above in this Section 2. To the extent that any such covenant shall be judicially unenforceable in any one or more of such states, such covenant shall not be affected with respect to each of the other states in the Territory. Each covenant with respect to each such state in the Territory shall be construed as severable and independent.
perturbation.changed_text: The covenants set forth in this Section 2 shall be construed as a series of separate covenants covering their subject matter in each of the separate states within the -2- <PAGE> Territory and, except for geographic coverage, each such separate covenant shall be deemed identical in terms to the covenant set forth above in this Section 2. To the extent that any such covenant shall be judicially unenforceable in any one or more of such states, such covenant shall *automatically invalidate the entire Section 2*. Each covenant with respect to each such state in the Territory shall be construed as severable and independent.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the severability clause to state that unenforceability in one state automatically invalidates the entire section makes the non-compete agreement much weaker. This is because the original agreement intended to be enforced across the United States, and now just one state can invalidate the entire agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 184 of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts deals with the enforceability of agreements with unlawful terms. It suggests that if only part of an agreement is unenforceable, a court should strive to enforce the rest, unless doing so would be contrary to the public interest or unless the party seeking to avoid enforcement can demonstrate that they wouldn't have agreed to the valid parts of the contract separately. Here, the altered text directly contradicts this principle. By stating the unenforceability in one state invalidates the entire agreement, the parties prevent a court from severing the unlawful part and enforcing the rest. This contradicts the idea of severability and creates an all-or-nothing situation which is very dangerous for the company and goes against the common practice of contract enforcement.

The promise of legality and fair procedure Historically, the clause reflects the Magna Ca
perturbation.justification: The altered text introduces a significant structural flaw by changing the severability clause. The original text ensures that if a covenant is unenforceable in one state, it doesn't affect its enforceability in other states. The changed text, however, stipulates that unenforceability in one state automatically invalidates the entire section. According to the Law Explanation, this directly contradicts the principle of severability which is that courts should strive to enforce the rest of an agreement unless it is against public interest. This makes the agreement much weaker by creating an all-or-nothing situation for the company. The altered text explicitly removes a key component of severability, creating a direct contradiction. The 'due process' snippet doesn't directly address the contradiction but indirectly, refers to the concept that laws (and by extension, contracts) should operate within the law and provide fair procedures. By making the entire agreement void due to one state's ruling, the altered text undermines this principle, as it could lead to unfair outcomes.
